meta.originalTitle: Caral an ancient South American city 卡拉尔古城

children.text: You should spend about 20 minutes on Questions 1–13, which are based on Reading Passage 1 below.
Caral: an ancient South American city

Huge earth and rock mounds rise out of the desert of the Supe Valley near the coast of Peru in South America. These immense mounds appear simply to be part of the geographical landscape in this arid region squeezed between the Pacific Ocean and the Andes mountains. But looks deceive. These are actually human-made pyramids. Strong evidence indicates they are the remains of a city known as Caral that flourished nearly 5,000 years ago. If true, it would be the oldest known urban center in the Americas and among the most ancient in the world.

Research undertaken by Peruvian archaeologist Ruth Shady suggests that the 150-acre complex of pyramids, plazas and residential buildings was a thriving metropolis when Egypt's great pyramids were still being built. Though discovered in 1905, for years Caral attracted little attention, largely because archaeologists believed the structures were fairly recent. But the monumental scale of the pyramids had long interested Shady, who began excavations at the site in 1996, about 22 kilometers from the coast and 190 kilometers north of Peru's capital city of Lima.

Shady and her crew searched for broken remains of the pots and containers that most such sites contain. Not finding any only made her more excited; it meant Caral could be what archaeologists term pre-ceramic, that is, existing before the advent in the area of pot-firing techniques. Shady's team undertook the task of excavating Piramide Mayor, the largest of the pyramids. After carefully clearing away many hundreds of years' worth of rubble and sand, they identified staircases, walls covered with remnants of colored woven bags and brickwork. In the foundations, they found the remains of grass-like reeds woven into bags. The original workers, she surmised, must have filled these bags with stones from a nearby quarry and laid them atop one another inside retaining walls, gradually giving rise to the pyramid's immense structure. Shady had samples of the reeds subjected to radiocarbon dating and found that the reeds were 4,600 years old. This evidence indicated that Caral was, in fact, more than 1,000 years older than what had previously been thought to be the oldest urban center in the Americas.

What amazed archaeologists was not just the age, but the complexity and scope of Caral. Piramide Mayor alone covers an area nearly the size of four football fields and is 18 meters tall. A nine-meter-wide staircase rises from a circular plaza at the foot of the pyramid, passing over three terraced levels until it reaches the top. Thousands of manual laborers would have been needed to build such a project, not counting the many architects, craftsmen, and managers. Shady's team found the remains of a large amphitheater, containing almost 70 musical instruments made of bird and deer bones. Clearly, music played an important role in Caral's society. Around the perimeter of Caral are a series of smaller mounds and various buildings. These indicate a hierarchy of living arrangements: large, well-kept rooms atop pyramids for the elite, ground-level quarters for craftsmen, and shabbier outlying dwellings for workers.

But why had Caral been built in the first place? Her excavations convinced Shady that Caral once served as a trade center for the region, which extends from the rainforests of the Amazon to the high forests of the Andes. Shady found evidence of a rich trading environment, including seeds of the cocoa bush and necklaces of shells, neither of which was native to the immediate Caral area. This environment gave rise to people who did not take part in the production of food, allowing them to become priests and planners, builders and designers. Thus occupational specialization, elemental to an urban society, emerged.

But what sustained such a trading center and drew travelers to it? Was it food? Shady and her team found the bones of small edible fish, which must have come from the Pacific coast to the west, in the excavations. But they also found evidence of squash, sweet potatoes and beans having been grown locally. Shady theorized that Caral's early farmers diverted the area's rivers into canals, which still cross the Supe Valley today, to irrigate their fields. But because she found no traces of maize, which can be traded or stored and used in times of crop failure, she concluded that Caral's trade leverage was not based on stockpiling food supplies.

It was evidence of another crop in the excavations that gave Shady the best clue to Caral's success. In nearly every excavated building, her team discovered evidence of cotton—seeds, fibers and textiles. Her theory fell into place when a large fishing net made of those fibers, unearthed in an unrelated dig on Peru's coast, turned out to be as old as Caral. "The farmers of Caral grew the cotton that the fishermen needed to make their nets,” Shady speculates. “And the fishermen gave them shellfish and dried fish in exchange for these nets." In essence, the people of Caral enabled fishermen to work with larger and more effective nets, which made the resources of the sea more readily available, and the fishermen probably used dried squash grown by the Caral people as flotation devices for their nets.
Do the following statements agree with the information given in Reading Passage 1?
In boxes 1–6 on your answer sheet, write
if the statement agrees with the information
if the statement contradicts the information
if there is no information on this
Caral was built at the same time as the construction of the Egyptian pyramids.
The absence of pottery at the archaeological dig gave Shady a significant clue to the age of the site.
The stones used to build Piramide Mayor came from a location far away.
The huge and complicated structures of Piramide Mayor suggest that its construction required an organised team of builders.
Archaeological evidence shows that the residents of Caral were highly skilled musicians.
The remains of housing areas at Caral suggest that there were no class distinctions in residential areas.

explanationSection.text:
答案：FALSE
解析：第二段说当埃及金字塔“were still being built”（仍在建造时），卡拉尔已经是一个“thriving metropolis”（繁荣的都市），说明卡拉尔的建成时间更早，而非同时。
答案：TRUE
解析：第三段说没有陶器意味着卡拉尔可能是“pre-ceramic”（前陶瓷时期），即年代更早，这是个关键线索。
答案：FALSE
解析：第三段推测工人们是用“stones from a nearby quarry”（附近的采石场）的石头来填充袋子。
答案：TRUE
解析：第四段提到建造需要“Thousands of manual laborers... not counting the many architects, craftsmen, and managers”，这表明了高度的组织化。
答案：NOT GIVEN
解析：第四段说发现了大量乐器，说明音乐“played an important role”，但这并不能证明居民的技艺是否“highly skilled”（高超）。
答案：FALSE
解析：第四段描述了不同等级的住房安排，表明存在“a hierarchy of living arrangements”（居住安排的等级制度），与“no class distinctions”相反。

items.text:
(1) 题目 1：Caral was built at the same time as the construction of the Egyptian pyramids. （卡拉尔的建造与埃及金字塔是同时期。）
答案：FALSE
解析：第二段说当埃及金字塔“were still being built”（仍在建造时），卡拉尔已经是一个“thriving metropolis”（繁荣的都市），说明卡拉尔的建成时间更早，而非同时。

(2) 题目 2：The absence of pottery at the archaeological dig gave Shady a significant clue to the age of the site. （发掘地未发现陶器让夏迪对遗址年代有重要线索。）
答案：TRUE
解析：第三段说没有陶器意味着卡拉尔可能是“pre-ceramic”（前陶瓷时期），即年代更早，这是个关键线索。

(3) 题目 3：The stones used to build Piramide Mayor came from a location far away. （建造主金字塔的石头来自很远的地方。）
答案：FALSE
解析：第三段推测工人们是用“stones from a nearby quarry”（附近的采石场）的石头来填充袋子。

(4) 题目 4：The huge and complicated structures of Piramide Mayor suggest that its construction required an organised team of builders. （主金字塔巨大而复杂的结构表明其建造需要有组织的团队。）
答案：TRUE
解析：第四段提到建造需要“Thousands of manual laborers... not counting the many architects, craftsmen, and managers”，这表明了高度的组织化。

(5) 题目 5：Archaeological evidence shows that the residents of Caral were highly skilled musicians. （考古证据显示卡拉尔的居民是技艺高超的音乐家。）
答案：NOT GIVEN
解析：第四段说发现了大量乐器，说明音乐“played an important role”，但这并不能证明居民的技艺是否“highly skilled”（高超）。

(6) 题目 6：The remains of housing areas at Caral suggest that there were no class distinctions in residential areas. （卡拉尔的住宅遗迹表明没有阶层差异。）
答案：FALSE
解析：第四段描述了不同等级的住房安排，表明存在“a hierarchy of living arrangements”（居住安排的等级制度），与“no class distinctions”相反。

children.text: from the passage for each answer.
Items discovered at Caral but not naturally occurring in the area
the remains of certain food such as
still in existence today indicate water diverted from rivers
Evidence of relationship with fishing communities
the excavation findings and fishing nets found on the coast suggest Caral farmers traded
dried squash may have been used to aid

explanationSection.text:
答案：seeds
解析：第五段提到发现了“seeds of the cocoa bush”。
答案：shells
解析：第五段提到了“necklaces of shells”。
答案：fish
解析：第六段提到了“the bones of small edible fish”。
答案：canals
解析：第六段说农民将河水引入“canals, which still cross the Supe Valley today”。
答案：maize
解析：第六段明确指出“she found no traces of maize”。
答案：cotton
解析：第七段的核心理论是卡拉尔农民种植并交易“cotton”给渔民。
答案：flotation
解析：第七段末尾推测渔民使用干南瓜作为渔网的“flotation devices”。

items.text:
(1) 题目 7：the 7 \___\___ of a certain plant （某种植物的7 \___\___）
答案：seeds
解析：第五段提到发现了“seeds of the cocoa bush”。

(2) 题目 8：8 \___\___ used to make jewellery （用来做首饰的8 \___\___）
答案：shells
解析：第五段提到了“necklaces of shells”。

(3) 题目 9：the remains of certain food such as 9 \___\___ （某些食物的残留物，如9 \___\___）
答案：fish
解析：第六段提到了“the bones of small edible fish”。

(4) 题目 10：10 \___\___ still in existence today indicate water diverted from rivers （仍然存在的10 \___\___表明水从河流改道）
答案：canals
解析：第六段说农民将河水引入“canals, which still cross the Supe Valley today”。

(5) 题目 11：no evidence that 11 \___\___ was grown （没有证据表明种植了11 \___\___）
答案：maize
解析：第六段明确指出“she found no traces of maize”。

(6) 题目 12：the excavation findings and fishing nets found on the coast suggest Caral farmers traded 12 \___\___ （发掘与海岸渔网表明卡拉尔农民交易了12 \___\___）
答案：cotton
解析：第七段的核心理论是卡拉尔农民种植并交易“cotton”给渔民。

(7) 题目 13：dried squash may have been used to aid 13 \___\___ of fishing nets （干南瓜可能用于帮助渔网的13 \___\___）
答案：flotation
解析：第七段末尾推测渔民使用干南瓜作为渔网的“flotation devices”。
